coredata.dc:description: Conventional photocatalysts utilized in the photodegradation of organic dyes are prone to cause agglomeration of nanocatalysts, which affects the photocatalytic efficiency and is difficult to recycle. In this paper, photocatalysts were rapidly loaded onto polypropylene (PP) meltblown nonwoven material fibers possessing a large specific surface area using water as a solvent and a one-step in situ growth method under ultrasonic conditions. That is, by ultrasonically and rapidly growing zeolite imidazolium skeleton material (ZIF-8) in situ on the surface of dopamine-modified PP meltblown fibers and directly doping carbon dots (CDs) modified TiO2 during the in situ growth of ZIF-8, a CDs-TiO2/ZIF-8/PP composite photocatalytic material was formed. The microstructure, chemical structure, and photocatalytic performance of CDs-TiO2/ZIF-8/PP composite photocatalytic material were characterized. The findings indicated that the CDs-TiO2/ZIF-8 heterojunction nanocatalysts were effectively loaded onto the exterior of PP meltblown fibers. The CDs-TiO2/ZIF-8/PP composite photocatalytic materials exhibited highly efficient and synergistic photocatalytic degradation of the anionic dye Congo red (CR). Upon exposure to visible light, the degradation of CR could reach 99.1 % within 100 min and 99.6 % within 120 min. The first-order kinetic constant for photocatalytic degradation was measured to be 0.0419 min⁻1. Furthermore, the photodegradation of CR by the CDs-TiO2/ZIF-8/PP composite photocatalytic material maintained about 85 % after 5 cycles. Finally, the mechanism of in situ deposition and photocatalytic activity of the nanocomposites on the fiber surface was proposed. The composite photocatalytic material can be recycled, and the preparation process is green, simple, and efficient, which has potential application prospects for industrial wastewater treatment.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic diagram of the preparation process of CDs-TiO2/ZIF-8/PP (a). XRD (b) and FT-IR (c) of PP, PDA/PP, ZIF-8/PP, TiO2/ZIF-8/PP, and CDs-TiO2/ZIF-8/PP; XPS survey spectra of ZIF-8/PP, TiO2/ZIF-8/PP, and CDs-TiO2/ZIF-8/PP (d) and high-resolution XPS spectra of N 1 s, Zn 2p and Ti 2p peaks (e-g).

SEM images of PP (a1), PDA/PP (a2), ZIF-8/PP (b1, b2), TiO2/ZIF-8/PP (c1, c2), and CDs-TiO2/ZIF-8/PP (d1, d2); TEM images of CDs-TiO2/ZIF-8/PP (e-f); HRTEM images, inverse FFT images, and line profile images (g); elemental quantitative analysis of CDs-TiO2/ZIF-8/PP (h) and elemental mapping images (i-n).

TG profiles of PP, PDA/PP, ZIF-8/PP, TiO2/ZIF-8/PP and CDs-TiO2/ZIF-8/PP (a); nitrogen adsorption isotherms (b) and pore size distributions (c) for PP, ZIF-8/PP and CDs-TiO2/ZIF-8/PP; PL spectra of ZIF-8/PP, TiO2/ZIF-8/PP, and CDs-TiO2/ZIF-8/PP samples (d); UV–vis spectra of PP, PDA/PP, ZIF-8/PP, TiO2/ZIF-8/PP, and CDs-TiO2/ZIF-8/PP (e); and the relationship curves between (αhv)2 of the prepared samples and photon energy (hv) relationship curves between (f).

Photocatalytic degradation of CR via different samples upon visible light irradiation (a); photocatalytic degradation of CR by different CDs-TiO2/ZIF-8/PP additions under visible light irradiation (b); photocatalytic degradation of CR by CDs-TiO2/ZIF-8/PP under visible light irradiation at different initial concentrations (c); photocatalytic degradation of CR under different pH values (d); Zeta potential method to determine the surface charge of CDs-TiO2/ZIF-8/PP at different pH values (e); changes in TOC and COD removal during photocatalytic degradation of CR (f); photocatalytic cyclic degradation experiments of CDs-TiO2/ZIF-8/PP on CR under visible light irradiation (g); XRD spectra of recovered CDs-TiO2/ZIF-8/PP after 5 cycles (h); SEM images of recycled CDs-TiO2/ZIF-8/PP after 5 cycles (i). (n = 3).

Photocatalytic degradation experiments of CR containing inorganic salts NaCl and Na2SO4 by CDs-TiO2/ZIF-8/PP under visible light irradiation (a); photocatalytic degradation experiments of different anionic dyes and cationic dyes by CDs-TiO2/ZIF-8/PP under visible light irradiation (b); the impact of the addition of different capture agents on the CDs-TiO2/ZIF-8/PP photocatalytic degradation of CR (c); EPR signals of DMPO-·O2 - (d) and DMPO-·OH (e) adducts over CDs-TiO2/ZIF-8/PP; photocurrent response of different samples over 6 cycles (f); EIS spectra of different samples (g); Mott-Schottky curves of ZIF-8 and TiO2 (h); energy band alignment diagrams of ZIF-8 and TiO2 (i). (n = 3).
Schematic illustration of the photocatalytic degradation mechanism of CR by CDs-TiO2/ZIF-8/PP.
The kinetics of CR degradation within different circumstances.
Comparison of the effects of CDs-TiO2/ZIF-8/PP and other fiber-based photocatalytic materials in degrading CR.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Conventional photocatalysts utilized in the photodegradation of organic dyes are prone to cause agglomeration of nanocatalysts, which affects the photocatalytic efficiency and is difficult to recycle. In this paper, photocatalysts were rapidly loaded onto polypropylene (PP) meltblown nonwoven material fibers possessing a large specific surface area using water as a solvent and a one-step in situ growth method under ultrasonic conditions. That is, by ultrasonically and rapidly growing zeolite imidazolium skeleton material (ZIF-8) in situ on the surface of dopamine-modified PP meltblown fibers and directly doping carbon dots (CDs) modified TiO2 during the in situ growth of ZIF-8, a CDs-TiO2/ZIF-8/PP composite photocatalytic material was formed. The microstructure, chemical structure, and photocatalytic performance of CDs-TiO2/ZIF-8/PP composite photocatalytic material were characterized. The findings indicated that the CDs-TiO2/ZIF-8 heterojunction nanocatalysts were effectively loaded onto the exterior of PP meltblown fibers. The CDs-TiO2/ZIF-8/PP composite photocatalytic materials exhibited highly efficient and synergistic photocatalytic degradation of the anionic dye Congo red (CR). Upon exposure to visible light, the degradation of CR could reach 99.1 % within 100 min and 99.6 % within 120 min. The first-order kinetic constant for photocatalytic degradation was measured to be 0.0419 min⁻1. Furthermore, the photodegradation of CR by the CDs-TiO2/ZIF-8/PP composite photocatalytic material maintained about 85 % after 5 cycles. Finally, the mechanism of in situ deposition and photocatalytic activity of the nanocomposites on the fiber surface was proposed. The composite photocatalytic material can be recycled, and the preparation process is green, simple, and efficient, which has potential application prospects for industrial wastewater treatment.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: No data was used for the research described in the article.

ce:section.ce:para: Among the many environmental pollution problems, organic dye wastewater discharged from the textile industry has become a major challenge in the realm of water purification due to its deep color, excellent chemical resistance, and difficult biological degradation [1,2]. Traditional physicochemical methods such as adsorption, precipitation, and oxidation often suffer from low treatment efficiency and secondary pollution, while photocatalytic technology is a potential solution for degrading pollutants due to its non-toxic or low toxicity and high efficiency [3–5]. Photocatalyst is the core of photocatalytic technology, but there are drawbacks to catalytic degradation of organic pollutants in water, such as agglomeration of catalyst powder, not easy to recycle, and secondary pollution [6,7]. For this reason, researchers have attempted to load photocatalysts onto large-scale, easy-to-recycle materials to solve the above problems. Among them, loading photocatalysts onto fibers or fabrics is of great research significance due to the advantages of convenience and cheapness [8–10].

So far, the photocatalysts loaded on fibers and fabrics include TiO2 nanoparticles, ZnO, chalcogenide materials, and bismuth-based compounds [11,12]. Among the various photocatalytic semiconductor materials, TiO2 having a bandgap of 3.2 eV is the most conventional photocatalytic semiconductor, extensively applied in wastewater treatment because of its thermal stability, non-toxic nature, and cost-effectiveness [13,14]. Sun et al. [15] loaded multilayers of TiO2 on a glass fiber cloth, and the photodegradation of crystalline violet reached 85 % under sunlight. However, TiO2 is limited by its structure, on the one hand, the small specific surface area makes it difficult to enrich the target. Conversely, the transient photogenerated electrons and photogenerated holes have short lifetimes and are easy to recombine, making it difficult to contact the target in time, thus affecting the photocatalytic efficiency. Modification of titanium dioxide can boost the photocatalytic performance of TiO2, such as modification of TiO2 by carbon dots (CDs) [16,17]. In addition, augmenting the surface area of photocatalysts offers more active sites and enhanced adsorptive capabilities, consequently elevating photocatalytic efficiency. Zeolite imidazolium skeleton material (ZIF-8) is a kind of metal-organic framework (MOF), and the combination of traditional semiconductor catalysts with MOFs to form heterostructures of photocatalysts has received extensive attention because of its elevated specific surface area, robust thermal and chemical stability, and enhanced adsorptive capacity [18]. For example, Guan et al. [19] assembled the photocatalyst Ag/AgCl/ZIF-8/TiO2 applied to cotton fabric surfaces via a straightforward soaking method, and the functional cotton fabrics exhibited efficient photodegradation of methylene blue (MB). Zhan et al. [20] first grew ZIF-8 in situ on the surface of polyacrylonitrile (PAN) fibers, then continued to load Ag/AgCl, and finally TiO2 was grown under high temperature and pressure to form ZIF-8/Ag/AgCl/TiO2/PAN composites. Thanks to the synergistic effect of ZIF-8, Ag/AgCl, and TiO2, the modified PAN nanofiber membrane exhibited high photocatalytic activity. In addition, Ran et al. [21] first loaded TiO2 on the surface of carbon fiber (CF) and then obtained ZIF-8/TiO2/CF composite photocatalytic materials by growing ZIF-8 in situ over the exterior of TiO2 in an autoclave. The loading of TiO2 and ZIF-8 onto fabrics by simple impregnation is simple and convenient, but the bonding between the photocatalysts and the fabrics is weak and prone to fall off during use. The loading of TiO2 and ZIF-8 onto fabrics by in-situ growth methods like these is accomplished step by step, i.e., the photocatalysts are loaded onto fabrics sequentially by sequential order. However, the preparation process of this method is complicated and has a long cycle time. Therefore, how to prepare composite photocatalytic materials by loading TiO2 and ZIF-8 onto fabrics through a simple and rapid method is an important prerequisite for developing fiber-based composite photocatalysts for diverse applications in photocatalysis.

Therefore, in this study, the photocatalyst TiO2 was simultaneously loaded with ZIF-8 on the surface of polypropylene (PP) meltblown nonwoven material fibers featuring a large surface area, utilizing water as a solvent and a one-step in-situ growth method under ultrasonic conditions. To solve the problem of easy shedding of the photocatalyst, the PP meltblown fibers were modified with dopamine to increase the reactive functional groups present on the surface of the PP meltblown fibers and supply reactive sites for direct attachment of ZIF-8. The specific process was to ultrasonically and rapidly grow ZIF-8 in situ on the surface of dopamine-modified PP meltblown fibers and to dope CDs-modified TiO2 in situ during the synthesis of ZIF-8 to form CDs-TiO2/ZIF-8/PP photocatalytic composites. The direct formation of ZIF-8 on the fiber's exterior was carried out by loading CDs-TiO2 into the pores and surface of ZIF-8 to form a heterojunction catalyst in situ on the fiber surface. The process was synthesized under a room-temperature ultrasonic environment using water as the solvent, which was used to accelerate the growth process of ZIF-8. The prepared CDs-TiO2/ZIF-8/PP photocatalytic composites were then subjected to a diverse array of characterization techniques encompassing X-ray diffractometry (XRD), Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FTIR), scanning electron microscopy (SEM), X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) and ultraviolet-visible (UV–vis) diffuse reflectance spectrometric analysis. Subsequently, the photocatalytic efficacy of the CDs-TiO2/ZIF-8/PP photocatalytic composites was assessed under visible light exposure with the aid of Congo red (CR) served as a test case, and the reusability of the CDs-TiO2/ZIF-8/PP photocatalytic composites was examined through repetitive testing. Ultimately, the potential degradation pathways and mechanisms of the synthesized photocatalysts were deduced from experiments involving radical scavengers.

Citric acid (99.5 %), urea (99 %), titanium dioxide (TiO2, 5–10 nm, anatase), dopamine hydrochloride (98 %), Tris-HCl buffer (pH=8.5), Congo red (CR), methyl orange (MO), methylene blue (MB), dimethylimidazole (2-MIM, 98 %), sodium hydroxide (NaOH, 95 %) and anhydrous ethanol (C2H6O, 99.5 %) were purchased from Shanghai McLean Biochemical Technology Co. Acid Orange 7 (AO7) and zinc nitrate hexahydrate (Zn(NO3)2·6 H2O, 99 %) were purchased from Shanghai Aladdin Biochemical Science and Technology Co. All chemical reagents could be used directly without further purification. PP meltblown nonwoven material was homemade in the laboratory with a grammage of 25 g/m2.

Modification of TiO2 by CDs (CDs-TiO2): A total mass of 9.0 g of citric acid and urea was sequentially added to 20.0 mL of deionized water, and the mass ratio of citric acid and urea was 1:2, respectively, and ultrasonication was conducted for a duration of 10 min at room temperature, and the mixed solution was placed into a microwave chemical reactor for 5 min at 650 W to form a yellow viscous paste or dark brown solid. Then add 20.0 mL of deionized water and centrifuge for 10 min (8000 rpm), take the supernatant and place it in a drying oven to dry at 80 °C, and then grind it to obtain the CDs powder sample. Then add 0.06 g of CDs and 1.0 g of TiO2 into 10.0 mL of deionized water in sequence, CDs is 6 % of the mass fraction of TiO2, ultrasonic at room temperature for 10 min, and dry at 105 ℃ in a vacuum oven for a duration of 12 h. The dehydrated sample was then placed into a mortar and ground into powder, and then the resulting powder was introduced into a furnace, and the programmed temperature rise was set at 5 ℃ / min heating to 300 °C for 3 h. Post-calcination, the resultant powder was thoroughly rinsed several times with deionized water before being dried, and the CDs-TiO2 composite photocatalyst was produced after grinding.

Polydopamine (PDA) modification of PP meltblown fibers surface (PDA/PP): 0.3 g of dopamine hydrochloride was mixed into 300 mL of Tri-HCl buffer solution at pH 8.5 and agitated until completely dissolved. Sample of PP meltblown nonwoven material with a size of 20 × 20 mm were cut, washed ultrasonically in deionized water, and subsequently dried in a blower oven at 60 ℃. The washed and dried PP meltblown nonwoven material sample was immersed in the prepared dopamine solution at ambient temperature. and ultrasonically impregnated for 2 h, the fiber surface was modified with polydopamine, and then dried in a forced-air drying oven at 60 ℃ to yield PDA/PP specimens.

In situ growth of ZIF-8 on the surface of PP meltblown fibers (ZIF-8/PP): 1.467 g of Zn(NO3)2·6 H2O was mixed with 100 mL of deionized water as solution A and 16.225 g of 2-MIM as solution B, respectively, and dissolved by ultrasonication at room temperature, with the mass ratio of Zn(NO3)2·6 H2O to 2-MIM being 1:40, and solution B was introduced drop by drop into a 5 mL of 1 mol/L NaOH solution to expedite the imidazole deprotonation. The poly(dopamine)-modified PP meltblown nonwoven material sample was put into solution A and ultrasonically impregnated for 5 min at room temperature to load zinc ions over the exterior of the fibers, and then the PP meltblown nonwoven material sample was put into solution B and ultrasonically impregnated for 5 min at room temperature, and ZIF-8 was directly formed on the PP surface meltblown fibers, and the sample was washed rinsed repeatedly with deionized water, followed by drying in a drying oven at 60℃ under air circulation, to produce ZIF-8/PP composite material.

The PP meltblown fibers surface was simultaneously loaded with TiO2, CDs-TiO2, and ZIF-8 (TiO2/ZIF-8/PP, CDs-TiO2/ZIF-8/PP): 1 g of TiO2 or CDs-TiO2 was added to solution B, which was allowed to be dissolved by ultrasonication at room temperature, and the other preparations were the same as those for the preparation of ZIF-8/PP composites is illustrated in Fig. 1a.

The D8 Advance X-ray diffractometer (XRD) was utilized for analysis the crystalline properties and crystallographic structure of the samples, using Cu Kα rays (= 0.15406 nm), an accelerating voltage of 35 kV, a current intensity of 40 mA, a scanning rate of 10 °/min, with a scan range (2θ) of 5°-70°; the Nicolet iS50 Fourier Transform Infrared Spectrometer (FTIR) was applied for infrared characterization testing of the samples, and the chemical composition and structure of the specimens were analyzed across the spectral region of 4000–400 cm−1 featuring a resolution of 0.09 cm−1; the surface topography and dimensions of the specimens were observed utilizing a Regulus 8100 cold field emission scanning electron microscope (SEM) operated in secondary electron mode at an acceleration voltage of 10 kV; the microcrystalline structure of the materials was visualized using a JEM-F200 field emission high-resolution transmission electron microscope (HRTEM) at an acceleration voltage of 200 kv, and elemental detection of the samples was performed utilizing an assembled EX-36250EDD EDS spectrometer at an energy resolution of 136 ev; the elements contained in the samples as well as the structures of the compounds were analyzed using a NEXSA-type X-ray photoelectron spectrometer (XPS) with an Al Kα X X-ray beam (1486.6 eV) as the excitation source, and the XPS data were calibrated using a C 1 s signal at 284.6 eV; the specific surface area and pore size distribution were determined using a fully automated physicochemical adsorbentiometer, Autosorb-iQ-C, and by N2 adsorption-desorption isotherms. Prior to testing, the samples were vacuum degassed at 120 ℃ for 6 h to remove surface adsorbates. The specific surface area was calculated by BET (Brunauer-Emmett-Teller) model and the pore size distribution was analyzed based on BJH (Barrett-Joyner-Halenda) method; the samples were analyzed for thermal stability using a STA Model 449F5 Comprehensive Thermal Analyzer (TG) under N2 at a ramp rate of 10℃/min over the temperature range of 30–800℃; the emission spectra of the samples were measured in the range of 470–800 nm using a FLS1000 steady-state fluorescence spectrometer; the ultraviolet-visible (UV–vis) diffuse reflectance spectra of the samples were obtained using a UH4150 UV–vis spectrophotometer with a polytetrafluoroethylene (PTFE) white plate as a reference sample, with a scanning range of 200–800 nm, and the bandgap values of the samples were obtained by the Tauc curve; the surface charge characteristics of the samples were analyzed using a SURPASS-3 Solid Surface Zeta Potentiostat at room temperature with an electrolyte of 1 mmol/L potassium chloride and a test pH range of 4–10.

Photodegradation experiments were conducted within a photoreactor setup. A magnetic stirrer was incorporated, operating at a speed of 300 r/min, to guarantee complete mixing during the reaction. The 300 W xenon lamp served as the illuminating source, providing an optical power density of 132 mW/cm2. The reactor was equipped with a 250 mL sandwich beaker and a recirculating water bath cooling system was employed to prevent water from evaporating and to keep the temperature at ambient levels. The lamp was positioned 25 cm above the solution's surface. The photocatalytic degradation of CR dye by the samples was determined by a photochemical reactor. The composite photocatalytic material was added to 50 mL of dye solution of a certain mass concentration, and after dark adsorption equilibrium (30 min), it was irradiated with visible light. At intervals of 15 minutes, a 5 mL aliquot of the solution was extracted, passed through a 0.22 µm pore-sized membrane filter, and then subjected to centrifugation at 10,000 r/min for a duration of 10 minutes. The supernatant was subsequently collected for absorbance measurement of CR at 498 nm using a UV-2700 UV-Vis spectrophotometer.

The impact of varying initial dye concentrations (10–50 mg/L), photocatalyst amounts (0.2–1.8 g/L), and initial pH levels (3−11) on dye degradation efficiency was assessed using the one-factor-at-rest (OFAT) approach over a period of 120 minutes. Firstly, 30 mg/L dye was used to investigate the effect of different photocatalyst dosages (0.2, 0.6, 1.0, 1.4, and 1.8 g/L). The role of the photocatalyst was investigated. Secondly, the impact of the starting dye concentration (10, 20, 30, 40, 50 mg/L) was investigated at the optimum dosage. Finally, after obtaining the ideal amount and starting dye concentration, experiments were carried out to investigate the influence of pH (3, 5, 7, 9 and 11). The solution's initial pH was adjusted with 1 mol/L NaOH and 1 mol/L HCl, respectively, and the pH was measured by a pH meter. Moreover, the sustainability and reusability of the composite photocatalysts were assessed. After the reaction, the samples were ultrasonically washed with ethanol three times to thoroughly eliminate the dye molecules adsorbed onto the catalyst's surface and then dry it at 60°C for 3 h for subsequent cycles of testing. The mechanism underlying photocatalysis was explored through experiments utilizing various scavengers to determine the reactive species involved in the dye degradation process. Under the same conditions, free radical capture experiments were performed on superoxide anion radical (O2 -), hole (h+), and hydroxyl radical (·OH) by adding 1 mM benzoquinone (BQ), 1 mM ammonium oxalate, and 1 mL of isopropanol (IPA) as free radical scavengers to the aqueous solution of dyestuffs, respectively. Each experiment was conducted on three separate occasions, and the outcomes were computed as the mean.

The loading of the heterojunction catalyst CDs-TiO2/ZIF-8 on PP meltblown fibers was examined through XRD tests. As illustrated in Fig. 1b, each sample has four distinctive peaks located near 2θ= 14.70°, 16.89°, 18.47°, and 21.80°, which are associated with the (110), (040), (130), (131), and (041) crystalline surfaces of PP, respectively [22]. The presence of PP diffraction peaks in all the samples proves that the catalyst's incorporation does not alter the crystallographic structure of PP. In the PDA/PP samples, no obvious reflection peaks attributable to PDA were detected because of the amorphous structure of PDA and the strong diffraction peaks of PP as well as the low PDA content over the exterior of PP nonwoven fibers. The XRD patterns of pure ZIF-8 and TiO2 are shown in Fig. S1a-b, which shows that pure ZIF-8 displays characteristic diffraction peaks at 2θ = 7.3°, 10.4°, 12.7°, 14.7°, 16.4°, and 18.0°, which is in agreement with the simulated standard pattern of ZIF-8 [23]. Pure TiO2 shows characteristic peaks at 2θ = 25.3°, 37.8°, 48.0°, 53.9°, 55.1°, and 62.7°, corresponding to anatase phase TiO2 [24]. The XRD patterns of the three samples, ZIF-8/PP, TiO2/ZIF-8/PP and CDs-TiO2/ZIF-8/PP, showed a characteristic peak at 2θ = 7.40°, which corresponds to the (011) crystalline surface of ZIF-8 [25]. The XRD patterns of two samples, TiO2/ZIF-8/PP and CDs-TiO2/ZIF-8/PP, have characteristic peaks at 2θ= 25.2°, corresponding to the (101) crystal plane of TiO2 [26]. In addition, no obvious diffraction peaks of CDs were noticeable within the XRD pattern of this sample of CDs-TiO2/ZIF-8/PP, this could potentially be attributed to the non-crystalline nature or minimal quantity of CDs [27]. The XRD outcomes suggest that the in situ-synthesized CDs-TiO2/ZIF-8 nanoparticles have been effectively integrated onto PP meltblown fibers.

The composite photocatalyst's chemical composition was examined through FT-IR spectroscopy. As depicted in Fig. 1c, the characteristic spectral bands attributed to PP at 2949, 2916, 2866, 2836, 1454, 1375, 1166, 972, 840, and 808 cm−1 are observed in every specimen. Furthermore, the bands at 2949 and 2866 cm−1 are indicative of -CH3 asymmetric and symmetric stretching modes, respectively, while the bands at 2916 and 2836 cm−1 are related to the asymmetric and symmetric stretching vibrations of -CH2. The distinctive peaks at 1454, 1375, and 1166 cm−1 are due to the bending vibrations of -CH3 and tertiary carbon, respectively. Meanwhile, the spectral bands at 972, 840, and 808 cm−1 are assigned to the out-of-plane bending of C-H bonds [28]. Compared with pristine PP, PDA/PP exhibited significant N-H/O-H stretching vibrations between 3500 and 3200 cm−1, attributed to the existence of phenolic hydroxyl groups on the surface of PP meltblown fibers following the self-polymerization of dopamine, and the peak at 1640 cm−1 corresponded to the superposition of the combined vibration of CC and N-H bending within the aromatic ring structure by the aromatic ring brought about by PDA, which confirms that the PDA on PP meltblown fibers was successfully modified [29,30]. In addition, the characteristic spectral bands at 3140, 1580, 759, and 420 cm−1 appearing in the ZIF-8/PP sample are typical of ZIF-8, serving as evidence that ZIF-8 was effectively formed directly on the surface of PP meltblown fibers [31]. Vibrational bands at 487 and 564 cm−1 that appeared in the TiO2/ZIF-8/PP samples represent the characteristic vibrations of Ti-O-Ti and N-Ti-O linkages, with the N-Ti-O bonds suggesting that TiO2 and ZIF-8 are not merely a physical blend but are instead chemically integrated [32]. The interaction of Ti4+ (the presence of which was revealed by the XPS analysis) with the imidazole group of ZIF-8 (2-MI) is the probable cause for the creation of the N-Ti-O bond. It is worth mentioning that compared with TiO2/ZIF-8/PP, CDs-TiO2/ZIF-8/PP showed new characteristic peaks in the range of 1000–1500 cm−1, which might be ascribed to the doping of CDs in TiO2 to produce C-O and CO groups, and the generation and strength of chemical bonds could result in an increase in the overall photocatalytic system's activity. In addition, the broad absorption band was shifted below 1000 cm−1, which indicated that Ti-O-C bonds were formed within the CDs-TiO2/ZIF-8/PP hybrid structures, and also suggested that the coupling between TiO2 and CDs was realized through the formation of Ti-O-C bonds [33,34]. The FT-IR analysis revealed that the in situ synthesized CDs-TiO2/ZIF-8 nanoparticles had been successfully deposited on PP fibers.

The elemental composition and chemical states of ZIF-8/PP, TiO2/ZIF-8/PP, and CDs-TiO2/ZIF-8/PP composite photocatalytic materials were investigated using XPS. As illustrated in Fig. S1a, the XPS spectrum of C 1 s are capable of being resolved into four distinct peaks, CC, C-C, C-N and C-O [35,36]. Three peaks were measured in the N 1 s spectrum of CDs-TiO2/ZIF-8/PP (Fig. 1e). The peak at 398.87 eV belongs to the Zn-N bond [37], the peak at 399.68 eV matches the N-H bond from PDA [38], and the peak at 400.78 eV could be ascribed to the N-Ti-O bond, suggesting that there is an interaction between ZIF-8 and TiO2 [39]. In Fig. S1d, the O 1 s spectrum of CDs-TiO2/ZIF-8/PP measured four peaks at 529.64 eV, 531.73 eV, 532.96 eV, and 533.93 eV, corresponding to O-Ti, O-Zn, OC, and O-C bonds, respectively, indicating the formation of TiO2 and ZIF-8 [40]. Furthermore, the peak magnitude at 531.73 eV exceeds the magnitude at 529.64 eV, indicating that there is more ZIF-8 on the surface of PP meltblown fibers than TiO2. As shown in Fig. 1f, characteristic peaks of Zn 2p3/2 and Zn 2p1/2 were observed in the high-resolution spectra of Zn 2p, which indicate that Zn exists in ZIF-8 in the + 2 valence state and forms a ligand bond with the nitrogen atom in the imidazole ring. This finding aligns with the Zn 2p signature of ZIF-8 nanostructures [41]. In Fig. 1g, the two peaks located at 458.91 eV and 464.7 eV belong to Ti 2p3/2 and Ti 2p1/2, respectively, which are consistent with anatase TiO2, confirming the presence of Ti4+ in TiO2 [42]. By comparing the XPS spectra of ZIF-8/PP, TiO2/ZIF-8/PP, and CDs-TiO2/ZIF-8/PP, we found that the incorporation of CDs and TiO2 led to a slight shift of the Zn 2p peak, which indicated the existence of electronic interactions between TiO2 and ZIF-8. These changes in binding energy can promote the separation and transfer of photogenerated carriers, which helps to improve the photocatalytic performance of the materials. These results also indicate the successful loading of CDs-TiO2 and ZIF-8 on the surface of PP fibers.

The microstructure of the samples was analyzed using SEM. The smooth surface of the pristine PP meltblown fibers observed in Fig. 2a1 and the rough surface of the PDA/PP samples in Fig. 2a2 is due to the formation of a PDA layer on the fiber surface through dopamine self-polymerization [43]. Given that the PDA layer exhibits a strong adhesive property and harbors hydroxyl functional groups capable of complexing with Zn2+ ions, the homogeneous in situ growth of ZIF-8 nanoparticles on the surface of the PP meltblown fibers is plainly visible in Fig. 2b2. The ZIF-8 nanoparticles have the shape of an ortho-dodecahedron with a particle size of about 200 nm. Also in Fig. 2c2, TiO2 nanoparticles with a particle size of 5 nm were added during the in-situ formation of ZIF-8 atop the surface of PP meltblown fibers, respectively, and the uniform distribution of TiO2 and ZIF-8 nanoparticles on the surface of PP meltblown fibers is evident, but the particle size of ZIF-8 becomes smaller. This may be because the added TiO2 covered the surface and internal pores of ZIF-8, thus suppressing the growth of ZIF-8 and diminishing its particle size. The same phenomenon was observed in Fig. 2d2 after doping with CDs-TiO2. All the nanoparticles were loaded relatively uniformly on the PP meltblown fibers without significant agglomeration. This structural characteristic enlarges the specific surface area of the nanoparticles, facilitating the absorption of light by the catalyst during the photocatalytic process and can improve the photocatalytic efficiency.

In addition, the internal structure and elemental distribution of the CDs-TiO2/ZIF-8/PP composites were further investigated using TEM images. In Fig. 2e, it can be observed that CDs-TiO₂ and ZIF-8 are evenly distributed and attached to the surface of PP fibers, with the particle size of ZIF-8 being approximately 100 nm. In Fig. 2f, it can be seen that CDs-TiO2 is tightly bound on the surface of ZIF-8, and CDs and TiO2 show a homogeneous discrete distribution in the composites, with the particle size of CDs particles being about 3 nm, while the particle size of TiO2 particles is about 5 nm. The homogeneous discrete distribution of CDs and TiO2 on the surface of ZIF-8 contributes to the improvement of the photogenerated carriers' separation efficiency, which leads to the enhancement of the photocatalytic performance. To some extent, the presence of ZIF-8 precludes the agglomeration of CDs-TiO2, thereby playing a vital role in the separation of photogenerated electron-hole pairs. The presence of CDs and TiO2 can be observed from Fig. 2g, both of which are well dispersed and without significant aggregation. The lattice structure is obtained by Fourier transform (FFT), and the lattice stripe spacings are 0.35 nm and 0.24 nm correspondingly, which coincides with the (101) intra-planar lattice of anatase-structured TiO2 and the (100) intra-planar lattice of graphene [44–46]. In addition, the quantitative analysis of various elements of the CDs-TiO2/ZIF-8/PP composites (Fig. h) as well as the elemental mapping images (Fig. 2i-n) indicate that the Zn and Ti elements are uniformly distributed on the surface of PP meltblown fibers, which verifies the presence of ZIF-8 and TiO2. All the above analyses showed that CDs-TiO2 was tightly bound in the surface and internal pores of ZIF-8 to form CDs-TiO2/ZIF-8 composite photocatalysts and CDs-TiO2/ZIF-8 was effectively and uniformly loaded upon the surface of PP meltblown fibers.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Thermal stability and optical properties analysis
ce:section.ce:para: The thermal stability properties of the prepared composites were evaluated via thermogravimetric analysis (TG). As illustrated in Fig. 3a, the weight of all the samples decreased dramatically between 350 °C and 500 °C due to thermal degradation of organic matter. Because PDA has an amorphous structure, PDA/PP has a similar decomposition behavior compared to pristine PP [47]. The PP and PDA/PP samples reached the maximum weight loss rate at 459.5 °C and 459.65 °C, respectively, while ZIF-8/PP, TiO2/ZIF-8/PP and CDs-TiO2/ZIF-8/PP samples reached the maximum weight loss rate at 461.13 °C, 461.33 °C and 465.5 °C, respectively. Observing Fig. 3a, one can find that the thermogravimetric curves of the samples loaded with ZIF-8 and TiO2 are higher than those of pristine PP in general [48]. Eventually at 800 °C, the residual masses of pristine PP and PDA/PP were 0.11 % and 1.36 %, respectively, while the residual masses of ZIF-8/PP, TiO2/ZIF-8/PP, and CDs-TiO2/ZIF-8/PP samples were 1.95 %, 3 % and 4.06 %, respectively. Thus the loaded catalysts can improve the thermal stability of PP meltblown fibers to different degrees. In addition, the nitrogen adsorption and desorption curves as well as the pore size distributions of PP, ZIF-8/PP, and CDs-TiO2/ZIF-8/PP are depicted in Fig. 3b-c, and the comparison of the unit surface area and average pore size is illustrated in Table S1. From Fig. 3c, it can be observed that micropores and mesopores appeared after ZIF-8 crystals were in situ grown on PP meltblown fibers; while the number of mesopores within the range of 5–10 nm increased remarkably after the introduction of CDs-TiO2. In Table S1, it is able to observed that the ZIF-8/PP sample has a large specific surface area, and even though the specific surface area shows a decreasing trend after the introduction of CDs-TiO2 compared to the ZIF-8/PP sample, it is still 7 times higher than the surface area per unit of the PP meltblown fiber sample. The results showed that CDs-TiO2/ZIF-8/PP composite photocatalytic materials were successfully prepared. This micro mesoporous structure of CDs-TiO2/ZIF-8/PP composite photocatalytic materials broadened the pore size distribution of PP meltblown fibers and increased their unitary surface expanse, which furnished additional adsorption loci for the substrates engaged in the photocatalytic reaction and could promote the photocatalytic reaction.

The optical traits of the fabricated photocatalytic composites were revealed by photoluminescence (PL) spectra. The PL spectra of ZIF-8/PP, TiO2/ZIF-8/PP, and CDs-TiO2/ZIF-8/PP when the excitation wavelength was 392 nm are shown in Fig. 3d. In photocatalytic active reactions, the separation and transfer efficacy of electron holes induced by photogenerated effects play a crucial part in determining the photocatalytic activity. Moreover, the aggregation of photoexcited electron-hole pairs is liable to dissipate energy and enhance PL emission. Regarding the steady-state PL spectra, it is commonly acknowledged that a lower PL intensity indicates a reduced rate of electron-hole complexation resulting from photogenerated effects, and correspondingly, a higher efficiency of photoreceptor charge transfer and separation [49,50]. The PL spectra of ZIF-8/PP show broadband emission within the span of 550–750 nm, attributed to the rapid complexation of ZIF-8 photogenerated electrons and holes. In contrast, TiO2/ZIF-8/PP and CDs-TiO2/ZIF-8/PP exhibited lower fluorescence intensities, which indicated that the compounding rate of photogenerated carriers was largely suppressed and the carrier separation was more efficient in TiO2/ZIF-8/PP and CDs-TiO2/ZIF-8/PP compared with ZIF-8/PP, and TiO2 could function as an electron acceptor to facilitate charge separation. The results show that the formation of CDs-TiO2/ZIF-8 heterostructure after the introduction of CDs-TiO2 into ZIF-8/PP can significantly improve the capacity for charge transfer and restrain the recombination rate of photogenerated electron-hole pairs.

The UV–vis diffuse reflectance spectroscopy was employed to probe into the optical absorption properties of the composite photocatalytic materials. As depicted in Fig. 3e, no obvious absorption peaks were detected within the span of 200–800 nm for the pristine PP, indicating that it has no UV–vis absorption capability. Upon the introduction of a PDA layer onto the surface of PP meltblown fibers, along with the in situ loading of ZIF-8 and TiO2, the absorptivity of these composites, PDA/PP, ZIF-8/PP, and TiO2/ZIF-8/PP, was improved in the range of 200–250 nm, but they still lacked absorptivity in the visible region. As expected, after the introduction of CDs in TiO2, the formation of CDs-TiO2/ZIF-8/PP composite photocatalytic materials, was significantly enhanced in both the UV and visible regions, forming a good synergistic interaction among CDs, TiO2, and ZIF-8. Based on the outcomes of the UV–visible spectra, the band gaps of diverse samples are calculable via the formula: (αhv) = K (hv-Eg)n/2. In this context, α represents the absorption coefficient, h stands for Planck's constant, v denotes the incident light frequency, K is the constant, and Eg refers to the band gap energy, respectively. The Eg of various composites is illustrated by plotting (αhv)2 against hv through Tauc curves, as depicted in Fig. 3f. Among them, the CDs-TiO2/ZIF-8/PP composite photocatalytic substance exhibits a bandgap value of 2.81 eV, which is significantly smaller as compared to other composites. This will facilitate the generation of photogenerated electron-hole pairs and enhance the photocatalytic efficiency of the material under visible light irradiation.

On this basis, the photocatalytic efficacy of CDs-TiO2/ZIF-8/PP beneath simulated visible light was investigated in detail. CR was chosen as a model in this paper because it represents a class of non-biodegradable dyes that have broad utilization in the textile trade. To investigate the degradation performance, the apparent rate constant of dye degradation was calculated according to the photocatalytic reaction satisfying the first-order reaction kinetic equation (Eq. 1) to evaluate the degradation performance of the photocatalytic materials, and the degradation rate was computed in line with the formula (Eq. 2) [51]. Table 1 enumerates the apparent reaction rate constants along with the squares of correlation coefficients (R2) corresponding to diverse samples under assorted factors. In the formula, C0 (mg/L) denotes the initial concentration of CR, Ct (mg/L) signifies the concentration of CR at the moment of t (min). The apparent reaction rate constant is represented by k
app , and the degradation rate is indicated by D (%).
(1)
Ln (C t /C 0) = －k app t
(2)
D = (1－C t /C 0) × 100%

The photocatalytic process of different composite photocatalytic materials beneath visible light rays is presented in Fig. 4a, with a dye concentration of 40 mg/L and the dosage of various photocatalytic materials of 1 g/L. Blank tests verified that, without the photocatalysts, CR merely underwent a slight degree of degradation under visible light irradiation, which indicated that the effect of photolysis was negligible. The degradation effectiveness of ZIF-8/PP reached only 52.3 % after 120 min, which was due to its lower optical properties. The degradation effectiveness of CDs-TiO2/ZIF-8/PP on CR reached 99.1 % after 100 min and 99.6 % after 120 min. As the catalytic time lengthened, the red hue of the CR solution gradually diminished until it turned nearly colorless (Fig. 4a), signifying that the CR solution had been fully degraded. Notably, the removal of CR from the solution by ZIF-8/PP was about 21.2 % during the dark adsorption process, which exceeded that of the CDs-TiO2/ZIF-8/PP composite photocatalyst. This may be because the CDs-TiO2 nanoparticles upon the surface of CDs-TiO2/ZIF-8/PP hindered the adsorption of CR by ZIF-8, but the concerted photocatalytic influence of CDs-TiO2 and ZIF-8 made CDs-TiO2/ZIF-8/PP have a stronger photocatalytic effect than other materials. First, ZIF-8 possesses a larger specific surface area, enabling it to adsorb a greater quantity of CR molecules. Second, the catalytic synergy arising from ZIF-8 and CDs-TiO2 nanoparticles produces effective electron transfer that gives CDs-TiO2/ZIF-8/PP excellent photocatalytic activity. Third, dopamine undergoes self-polymerization on the exterior of PP fibers, leading to the formation of black polydopamine, which could absorb heat and accelerate the catalytic efficiency. The used CDs-TiO2/ZIF-8/PP was dried and weighed, and it was found that its quality did not change from that before use, indicating that the CDs-TiO2/ZIF-8 heterojunction was firmly bonded to the PP fiber.

To gain insights into the degradation kinetics of CR, a pseudo-first-order model was employed. The apparent rate constant can be derived from the slope of the linear correlation. As can be seen in Fig. S2a, the degradation time shows a good linear relationship with ln(C0/Ct), indicating that the degradation process of CDs-TiO2/ZIF-8/PP with respect to CR complies with the quasi-first-order kinetic model. The apparent rate of PP, PDA/PP, ZIF-8/PP, TiO2/ZIF-8/PP, and CDs-TiO2/ZIF-8/PP system constants (kapp) were 0.00112, 0.00173, 0.00553 and 0.01726, respectively (Table 1), which further demonstrated that CDs-TiO2/ZIF-8/PP had the best photocatalytic activity. Meanwhile, the degradation efficiency of CR by CDs-TiO2/ZIF-8/PP prepared in this experiment was compared with other reported fiber-based photocatalytic materials, and the outcomes are listed in Table 2. The findings indicated that the degradation efficiency of CDs-TiO2/ZIF-8/PP composite photocatalytic materials for CR excelled that of other photocatalyst materials reported previously. In conclusion, the analysis shows that the photocatalytic degradation of dyes by PP fibers loaded with CDs-TiO2/ZIF-8 heterojunctions is significantly improved and is expected to be used for dyeing wastewater treatment.

The degradation efficiency of CR subsequent to the addition of varying amounts of CDs-TiO2/ZIF-8/PP, with the dye concentration set at 40 mg/L, is illustrated in Fig. 4b. The degradation efficiency of CR grew in tandem with the rise in the dosage of CDs-TiO2/ZIF-8/PP. When the dosage of CDs-TiO2/ZIF-8/PP was greater than 1 g/L, the photocatalytic system was saturated, and the degradation efficiencies of CR could all reach more than 99 % after 120 min, consequently, the optimal dosage of the photocatalyst was determined to be 1 g/L. The degradation rate of CR could reach more than 99 % at 60 min with the addition of CDs-TiO2/ZIF-8/PP at 1.8 g/L. It took 90 min with the addition of CDs-TiO2/ZIF-8/PP at 1 g/L. The variation of ln(Ct/C0) with time for different additions of CDs-TiO2/ZIF-8/PP is shown in Fig. S2b. The impact of adding CDs-TiO2/ZIF-8/PP at a dosage of 1 g/L on the degradation of CR with diverse initial concentrations over a period of 120 min is illustrated in Fig. 4c. It is observable that the CR concentration in the solution dropped rapidly during the initial 45 min of the photocatalytic assay. This could be ascribed to the abundant active sites of the material. CR is rapidly adsorbed onto the surface of the material and subsequently undergoes photodegradation. As CR degrades on the surface, new active sites are freed up, enabling them to continuously degrade the remaining CR in the solution until complete degradation of CR is achieved. Upon completion of 120 min of the experiment, the degradation rate of CDs-TiO2/ZIF-8/PP could reach more than 99 % for CR solutions with concentrations of 40 mg/L or less. The kinetics of photocatalytic degradation of CDs-TiO2/ZIF-8/PP was also analyzed for various initial concentrations of CR (Fig. S2c and Table 1). The reaction rate did not decrease significantly when the initial concentration of CR was raised from 10 mg/L to 20 mg/L, and it was optimal when the initial concentration of CR was 20 mg/L. When the initial concentration of CR was increased to more than 30 mg/L, the reaction rate decreased significantly. This is because the photon penetration decreases upon excessive substrate concentration [55].

Given that dye wastewater invariably exhibits diverse pH values, it is highly significant to evaluate the influence of pH on the photodegradation activity of CDs-TiO2/ZIF-8/PP. In this paper, the photodegradation efficiencies of CR solution with a starting concentration of 20 mg/L and 1 g/L of CDs-TiO2/ZIF-8/PP composite photocatalytic material were investigated under acidic and alkaline conditions. The pH of the CR solution was regulated with HCl or NaOH solution (both at a concentration of 0.1 mol/L). As depicted in Fig. 4d, the photocatalytic degradation of CR dye molecules was achieved by CDs-TiO2/ZIF-8/PP in the range of pH from 3 to 11 and displayed the peak photocatalytic activity at pH= 5, and its photodegradation efficiency was 99 % after 120 min. Increasing or decreasing the solution pH led to a decline in the dye degradation rate, which could which was ascribable to that higher concentrations of H+ or OH- inhibited the adsorption of dyes on the surface of CDs-TiO2/ZIF-8/PP, leading to a significant decline in the dye degradation rate. The high photodegradation efficiency of CR at pH= 5 can be explained by the fact that in a weakly acidic environment, electrons can easily react with O2 to form H2O2, which is capable of further reacting with ·OH, the principal active substance during the photocatalytic process [56,57]. Overall, the weak acidic environment was most favorable for the photodegradation of CR mediated by CDs-TiO2/ZIF-8/PP under visible light. Fig. S2d shows the quasi-primary kinetics of CR degradation by CDs-TiO2/ZIF-8/PP at different pH values. The highest k aap value of 0.04202 was observed upon the aqueous solution pH= 5 (Table 1). The surface charge of CDs-TiO2/ZIF-8/PP was determined by the zeta potential method at different pH values. As depicted in Fig. 4e, the zeta potential of CDs-TiO2/ZIF-8/PP exhibited a positive value in an acidic environment and a negative one in an alkaline environment. The photocatalytic reaction took place at the interface between the material surface and water. When the pH was below 7, a large number of CR molecules were adsorbed by CDs-TiO2/ZIF-8/PP. However, when the pH exceeded 7, CR and CDs-TiO2/ZIF-8/PP repelled each other, which diminished the absorbability of the material.

In addition, to assess the degree of CR mineralization, we used a Shimadu TOC-L CPH analyzer (TC-IC mode) for total organic carbon (TOC) and a DR 1010 COD Rapid Determination Instrument for chemical oxygen demand (COD) (Fig. 4f). The results showed that the removal of TOC and COD was only 17.63 % and 28.91 % at 120 min of light exposure. In the process after 120 min, TOC and COD decreased rapidly. At 300 min, the removal of TOC and COD reached 71.55 % and 81.28 %. Within 120 min is mainly the decolorization stage, where the multiple chemical bonds in the dye molecules associated with the chromogenic groups are destroyed and mainly acidic organic matter is produced. The small changes in TOC and COD during the decolorization phase indicate that the dye molecules, although destroyed, are not completely mineralized to CO2. After 120 min, the organic acid molecules will be mineralized to CO2. Due to the presence of diazonium groups in the CR, the mineralization of CR then requires the disruption of the entire molecular structure, involving the breakage of a single bond, a process which is more difficult [58,59].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Recycling experiments of photocatalysts and reusability implications
ce:section.ce:para: The photocatalyst was loaded onto the surface of PP meltblown fibers and recycled without the need for centrifugation or filtration. The reusability and steadiness of this composite photocatalytic material are crucial for the degradation of actual water pollutants, for which recycling experiments were conducted. As presented in Fig. 4g, the CDs-TiO2/ZIF-8/PP composite photocatalytic material after the photocatalytic experiment was ultrasonically washed with ethanol three times to thoroughly remove the adsorbed dyes on the surface of the catalyst material. Subsequently, it was dried at 60 °C for 3 hours to prepare for the cycling experiment. After five successive cycle experiments, the degradation rate of CR remained at a high level, with the figure still reaching 90 %. This signified that CDs-TiO2/ZIF-8/PP possessed favorable regeneration performance. SEM and XRD tests also confirmed this result. The XRD spectra of the recycled CDs-TiO2/ZIF-8/PP were additionally documented in Fig. 4h, which maintained the characteristic peaks of CDs-TiO2/ZIF-8/PP, with no new peaks emerging, and there was no obvious change in its structure and composition. The photocatalytic reaction left the morphology of CDs-TiO2/ZIF-8/PP unaltered (Fig. 4i), which suggests that CDs-TiO2/ZIF-8/PP enjoys favorable stability and reusability. The excellent steadiness of this composite photocatalytic material is largely attributed to PDA, which harbors an abundant quantity of phenolic hydroxyl functional groups. These groups possess the ability to chelate with metal ions, thus facilitating the in situ growth of ZIF-8 on the fiber surface. In addition, PDA exhibits robust adhesion capabilities, enabling it to anchor the nanoparticles onto the surface of PP meltblown fibers. Therefore, the CDs-TiO2/ZIF-8/PP composites not only display excellent photocatalytic performance but also demonstrate good recyclability, and this dual feature is of particular significance for practical applications.

All of the aforementioned experiments used deionized water as the matrix background, however, the actual water matrix background is typically more intricate, incorporating a diverse range of natural organic matter and inorganic salt ions. Moreover, the varieties and concentrations of water pollutants differ substantially during the various processes of water treatment. Therefore, various water backgrounds exert diverse impacts on the efficacy of photocatalytic pollutant degradation. To conduct a further exploration of the photocatalytic treatment efficacy of CDs-TiO2/ZIF-8/PP towards printing and dyeing wastewater, the inorganic salts NaCl and Na2SO4, which are commonly used in the dyeing process, were incorporated into the CR dye for the degradation experiments at a dosage of 10 g/L, respectively. As depicted in Fig. 5a, the degradation rate of CR dye was determined to decrease from 94.4 % to 75.4 % and 69.3 %, respectively. The possible reason for this could be that both Cl- and SO4 2- consume the active species produced in the system and the inorganic salts also exacerbate the phenomenon of aggregation of dye molecules in solution, which is not conducive to the occurrence of catalytic degradation reaction. The degradation experiments of CDs-TiO2/ZIF-8/PP on different anionic dyes such as Methyl Orange (MO) and Acid Orange 7 (AO7), and cationic dyes such as Methylene Blue (MB) were also tested under the same conditions as those for deionized water. The impact of CDs-TiO2/ZIF-8/PP on the degradation of diverse dyes is illustrated in Fig. 5b. The results showed that CDs-TiO2/ZIF-8/PP degraded different dyes under visible light and degraded anionic dyes better than cationic dyes. This is mainly attributed to the electrostatic interactions between the catalyst surface charge and the dye molecules, as well as the structural properties of the anionic dye molecules themselves. This may be attributed to the following factors: first, we measured the zeta potential of CDs-TiO2/ZIF-8/PP (Fig. 4e), which showed that it was positively charged at pH= 7, and there was a strong electrostatic attraction with the anionic dyes, which was favorable for the adsorption and enrichment of the dye molecules on the catalyst surface. Secondly, anionic dye molecules usually contain more conjugated structures and active sites, which are more susceptible to attack by photogenerated holes and free radicals [60]. It can also be seen in Fig. 5b that the adsorption conditions of different dyes in the dark reaction were better for anionic dyes than for cationic dyes. The electrostatic repulsion between the cationic dyes and the catalyst surface reduces the adsorption efficiency of the dye molecules on the catalyst surface, thus affecting the degradation effect [61]. This technique is expected to be applied in the future to solve the pollution problem of different dyes in water.

To explore the photocatalytic mechanism of CDs-TiO2/ZIF-8/PP, various kinds of active scavengers were incorporated into the CR solution. BQ, ammonium oxalate, and IPA served as active agents to trap superoxide radicals (·O2⁻), holes (h⁺), and hydroxyl radicals (·OH) in turn. which play a crucial role in degrading organic pollutants. The experimental procedure was identical to that of the photodegradation experiment, with the only difference being the addition of scavengers. The outcomes are depicted in Fig. 5c, in which the degradation rates dropped to 63.83 %, 82.62 %, and 69.41 % upon the addition of benzoquinone, ammonium oxalate, and isopropanol, respectively, in comparison with the photocatalytic process free of any scavengers, this indicates that the photogenerated holes (h⁺), hydroxyl radicals (·OH), and superoxide radicals (·O2⁻) all played crucial roles in the photocatalytic process. Among them, the degradation efficiency was significantly reduced by the addition of benzoquinone and isopropanol. In addition, we analyzed by electron paramagnetic resonance (EPR) in order to study more comprehensively the generation and mechanism of action of reactive oxygen radicals during photocatalysis (Fig. 5d-e). In the dark, neither DMPO-·O2 - nor DMPO-·OH could detect the EPR signal. After 5 min and 10 min of visible light irradiation, the characteristic peaks of DMPO-·O2 - adducts could be detected, indicating the presence of ·O2 - radicals in this photocatalytic system. Similarly, the typical signals detected for DMPO-·OH adducts represent the ·OH active radicals produced in this photocatalytic system. Considering the consistent EPR and trapping results, we can conclude that ·O2 - and ·OH are the main active substances involved in the photocatalytic degradation of CR by CDs-TiO2/ZIF-8/PP.

We photoelectrochemically analyzed the samples and tested the separation of electron-hole pairs. Fig. 5f shows the transient photocurrent response versus light irradiation time for all samples for six cycles. It can be seen that PP and ZIF-8/PP have a very weak photocurrent response in each cycle, while the photocurrent density increases significantly when CDs-TiO2 is deposited on the surface of ZIF-8. The high transient photocurrent response of CDs-TiO2/ZIF-8/PP suggests that it has a high electron-transferring ability to efficiently separate photoelectron-hole pairs. Fig. 5g shows the electrochemical impedance spectra (EIS) of all the samples. As can be seen from the figure, the charge transfer resistance of CDs-TiO2/ZIF-8/PP was significantly lower than that of the other materials, indicating that the catalyst has the fastest charge migration rate at the inner surface. Therefore, the formation of CDs-TiO2/ZIF-8 heterojunction can realize faster charge transfer from the catalyst surface to the active site, which can improve the catalytic degradation efficiency of the substrate. The photocurrent and EIS results were in good agreement with the photocatalytic degradation CR results.

The flat band potential (Efb) of ZIF-8 and TiO2 was estimated by determining the Mott-Schottky (MS) curve. The positive slopes observed from the MS curves reveal the n-type semiconductor properties of ZIF-8 and TiO2 [62]. The Efb of ZIF-8 and TiO2 can be obtained from the tangent intercepts of the X-axis in the MS curves, which are −3.01 and −0.48 V vs. Ag/AgCl, respectively. Thus, the conduction band potentials (Ecb) of ZIF-8 and TiO2 are −2.81 and −0.28 eV, while the CBs of ZIF-8 and TiO2 are −2.81 and −0.28 V vs. NHE, respectively. In addition, in combination with the band gap values (Eg) obtained from their optical analyses (Fig. S3b), their valence-band potentials (Evb) are 2.27 and 2.9 eV, respectively (Evb=Ecb+Eg), and their VBs are 2.27 and 2.9 V vs. NHE. The energy band positions of ZIF-8 and TiO2 are shown in Fig. 5i. The probable photocatalytic mechanism of CDs-TiO2/ZIF-8/PP is illustrated in Fig. 6. Under continuous light irradiation when ZIF-8 and TiO2 (3.2 eV) nanoparticles are irradiated with photon energies greater than or equal to their bandgap energies, the electrons in the valence band will be activated to leap to the conduction band, thereby generating holes (h⁺) in the valence band, and an equal amount of electrons (e-) appearing on the conduction band, and the electron-hole pairs formed (Eq. 3), which have a strong redox capacity. Given that the conduction band position of ZIF-8 is higher than that of TiO2, a portion of electrons (e-) migrate from the conduction band of ZIF-8 to that of TiO2, while holes (h+) shift from the valence band of TiO2 to the valence band of ZIF-8. Meanwhile, there is an electronic coupling between the Π orbitals of CDs and the conduction band of TiO2, and subsequently the electrons can be transferred from TiO2 to the surface of CDs, thereby suppressing the recombination of electrons and holes. Subsequently, the electrons in the conduction band are captured by the adsorbed O2, leading to the generation of ·O2⁻ (Eq. 4), while the generated ·O2 − as well as the holes on the valence band capture H2O, adsorbed onto the surface of the material and then react to produce ·OH (Eqs. 5–6). Due to the direct oxidation reaction of CR molecules in the presence of holes, ·O2 − and ·OH radicals, CR molecules eventually degrade to CO2 and H2O (Eqs. 7–9) [63–65].

During the photodegradation process: (1) Owing to the space-framework structure of ZIF-8, which features a large specific surface area and robust adsorption properties, CR molecules and oxygen are adsorbed onto the photocatalyst CDs-TiO2/ZIF-8, thereby prolonging the contact process. During the photodegradation process, the adsorption equilibrium was disrupted as a result of the decomposition of the adsorbed CR. Consequently, CR would swiftly migrate from the solution to the catalyst. The highly efficient adsorption process remarkably expedited the enrichment of CR on the catalyst surface, thereby enhancing the photodegradation performance of the catalyst. (2) There is a synergistic effect between ZIF8, TiO2, and CDs nanoparticles. The formation of Type-II heterojunction between ZIF8 and TiO2, as well as the coupling effect of CDs and TiO2, can promote the electron-hole transfer among different energy levels, which can enhance the separation efficiency of electron-hole pairs and preclude the recombination of photoelectron-hole pairs. (3) The incorporation of CDs broadens the photoresponsive range of the photocatalyst from the UV region to the visible region, thus improving the utilization of visible light. In conclusion, the CDs-TiO2/ZIF-8/PP photocatalytic materials have excellent photocatalytic efficacy.
(3) TiO2 + ZIF-8 + hv → e- + h+
(4) e- + O2 → ·O2 -
(5) 2·O2 - + 2H2O → 2·OH + 2OH + 1O2
(6) h+ + H2O → ·OH + H+
(7) ·OH + CR → CO2 + H2O
(8) ·O2 - + CR → CO2 + H2O
(9) h+ + CR → CO2 + H2O

In this work, the photocatalyst CDs-TiO2/ZIF-8 was grown in-situ on PP meltblown nonwoven material featuring a large specific surface area by using a green and solvent-free strategy and one-step in-situ growth method in a room-temperature ultrasonic environment. Characterization results such as SEM, XRD, and XPS measurements verified the loading of CDs-TiO2/ZIF-8 on PP meltblown fibers, respectively. The CDs-TiO2/ZIF-8/PP composite photocatalytic materials exhibited highly efficient and synergistic photocatalytic degradation of anionic dyes, such as CR, and the degradation rate of CR under visible-light illumination could attain 99.6 % within 120 min. The first-order kinetic constant for the photocatalytic degradation was 0.0419 min⁻¹ . Furthermore, the CDs-TiO2/ZIF-8/PP composite photocatalytic material retained approximately 85 % of its photocatalytic degradation efficiency for CR even after 5 cycles. This CDs-TiO2/ZIF-8/PP composite photocatalytic material is capable of forming N-Ti-O chemical bonds, with charge transfer taking place between photoexcited TiO2 and ZIF-8, and electron-hole pair binding is inhibited. In addition, the excellent adsorption property of ZIF-8 ensures full contact between the catalyst and the dye to improve the photocatalytic efficiency. Therefore, this innovative photocatalytic composite not only precludes the prompt recombination of photogenerated electron-hole pairs but also surmounts the negative impacts on the catalytic performance caused by the aggregation of nanomaterials within the photocatalytic system. The preparation process takes only 10 min to complete, and the method is highly efficient, low-cost, and simple. The CDs-TiO2/ZIF-8/PP composite photocatalytic material solves the problems of catalysts that are inefficient, prone to agglomeration, and difficult to recycle and provides a promising strategy for photocatalytic applications, with potential applications in industrial wastewater treatment.
